food_subgroup: Fried potato snacks with Japanese seasoning and condiment

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for nori-seasoned fries specifically; closest parent 'french fries food product' (FOODON_00002694) used as anchor. Nori seasoning and wasabi aioli are not captured by this term.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers fried potato products as a category. No specific code exists for nori-seasoned fries with wasabi aioli dipping sauce.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept exists for this fusion snack food.

preparation_method: served with wasabi aioli dipping sauce

serving.description: 1 serving portion as served at restaurant
serving.natural_language_portion: One restaurant serving of nori fries with wasabi aioli is approximately 200 g, comprising a basket of deep-fried potato strips seasoned with nori powder and accompanied by a wasabi aioli dipping sauce.

nutrient_source.title: French Fries (deep-fried, salted) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; Nori (dried seaweed) — USDA FoodData Central; Wasabi — USDA FoodData Central; Mayonnaise — USDA FoodData Central. Composite estimate weighted by typical recipe proportions: ~85% fries, ~8% nori seasoning contribution, ~7% wasabi aioli.
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for nori fries with wasabi aioli as a composite dish. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: french fries (FDC ID 170720, deep-fried, salted), dried nori seaweed (FDC ID 168456), wasabi root (FDC ID 170073), and mayonnaise (FDC ID 172741). Proportions estimated as 80 g potato fries base + 5 g nori seasoning + 15 g wasabi aioli per 100 g composite. Energy and macros are weighted composites. Sodium elevated due to seasoning and aioli. Values represent a reasonable central estimate; actual restaurant preparation will vary.

gi_source.title: French fries GI data — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283. French fries (deep-fried): GI approximately 63 (medium). Wasabi aioli contributes negligible carbohydrate and does not materially alter GI.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 63 sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database and the International Tables of GI Values (Atkinson et al., 2008) for deep-fried french fries. Nori seasoning adds negligible carbohydrate. Wasabi aioli is a fat-dominant condiment and does not significantly alter the glycemic response of the dish. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (63 × 33.5 × 2.0) / 100 = 42.1 for a 200 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from seasoning, nori, and wasabi aioli
hypertension.guidance: At approximately 420 mg sodium per 100 g (840 mg per 200 g serving), a single serving provides over one-third of the 2,300 mg daily sodium limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; those with hypertension should limit portion size or avoid this dish.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium-to-high glycemic index (GI 63) and high carbohydrate load from deep-fried potato
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of 63 and a glycemic load of approximately 42 per 200 g serving, nori fries represent a high glycemic load food that can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume only small portions infrequently and pair with low-GI foods or protein to blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat and elevated sodium; deep-frying adds total fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Deep-frying contributes approximately 16.5 g total fat per 100 g including 2.4 g saturated fat, and the high sodium content (420 mg/100 g) is unfavourable for cardiovascular health; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption and prefer baked potato alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nori Fries with Wasabi Aioli is a Japanese-fusion snack dish consisting of deep-fried potato strips (french fries) generously seasoned with nori powder — finely ground dried Japanese seaweed — and served alongside a wasabi aioli dipping sauce made from mayonnaise, wasabi paste, and garlic. The dish originated as a creative fusion of Japanese umami-rich ingredients with the universally popular format of french fries, and is commonly found in fusion restaurants and gastropubs across Singapore and other Southeast Asian cities. The fries are typically crispy on the outside and fluffy inside, with the nori seasoning imparting a savoury, oceanic umami flavour. The wasabi aioli provides a creamy, pungent, and mildly spicy counterpoint. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense at approximately 295 kcal per 100 g, with a significant carbohydrate contribution (33.5 g/100 g) from the potato base and a moderate-to-high fat content (16.5 g/100 g) from deep-frying and the aioli. Sodium is elevated at approximately 420 mg per 100 g due to seasoning and the condiment. The glycemic index is approximately 63 (medium), making this a high glycemic load food per serving. Clinically, this dish is of concern for individuals managing hypertension (high sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (medium-high GI and high GL), and cardiovascular disease (total fat and sodium). Nori contributes trace iodine, vitamin K, and dietary fibre. The dish is vegetarian but not vegan due to egg in the mayonnaise. It is not suitable for those with egg or soy allergies without modification.
common_user_queries: How many calories in nori fries with wasabi aioli?
Is wasabi aioli high in fat?
What is the glycemic index of french fries?
What does nori taste like on fries?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for nori fries with wasabi aioli as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for french fries, dried nori, wasabi, and mayonnaise using weighted proportions. GI is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for deep-fried french fries as the closest analogous food. Confidence is medium because the composite estimation introduces uncertainty, particularly for sodium and fat content which vary significantly by restaurant preparation method.
notes: 1. Cuisine tag in input JSON was 'Italian / Pizza / Pasta' — this is incorrect for this dish. The dish is Japanese-fusion and has been reclassified accordingly. The field survey restaurant 'Mingle - Funan' appears to serve a fusion menu; the Italian cuisine tag likely reflects the restaurant's primary cuisine rather than this specific dish. 2. Vitamin B12 is -1 as it is not reliably reported in the USDA FoodData Central component sources used for this composite estimate; plant-based nori may contain trace B12 analogues but these are not bioavailable and are not counted. 3. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component sources for this composite. 4. All other -1 values in per_100g have been replaced with estimated numeric values from USDA component data. 5. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (63 × 33.5 g carbohydrate × 200 g serving) / (100 × 100) = 42.2, rounded to 42.1. 6. Halal status is marked false as wasabi aioli contains egg-based mayonnaise from unverified halal-certified sources; restaurant-specific halal certification would need to be confirmed. 7. Price SGD 17.00 noted from field survey source for contextual reference only; not used in nutritional calculations. 8. Multilingual requirements for Singaporean dishes (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) have been added based on the 'field survey data' source, correcting the previous iteration's assumption that only Japanese was mandatory for a Japanese-fusion dish.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Corrected cuisine from 'Italian' to 'Japanese-fusion' based on dish composition analysis.
Iteration 1: Built composite nutrient estimate from USDA FoodData Central component data for french fries, nori, wasabi, and mayonnaise.
Iteration 1: Sourced GI 63 from University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 for deep-fried french fries as closest analogous food.
Iteration 1: Added Japanese local name and romanized form as mandatory for Japanese-origin dish.
Iteration 1: Added Chinese simplified and traditional names for Singapore market context.
Iteration 1: Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_b12_mcg, vitamin_d_mcg) in notes with explicit reasons.

Iteration 2: Addressed REJECT status from previous iteration by re-evaluating multilingual requirements. Based on 'field survey data' source, the dish is treated as a Singaporean context dish, requiring full Singaporean multilingual coverage (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi).
Iteration 2: Researched and added Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names and aliases for 'Nori Fries with Wasabi Aioli'.
Iteration 2: Updated `local_names`, `aliases`, and `aliases_by_language` to include the new multilingual entries.
Iteration 2: Verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases list — all strings confirmed present verbatim.
Iteration 2: Confirmed GI 63 correctly classified as 'medium' (56-69 range per validator thresholds).
Iteration 2: Confirmed all ontology match_type values use only allowed strings: exact, closest_parent, none.
Iteration 2: Verified no zero core macros requiring justification phrases.
